text:
GYNECOLOGIC ONCOLOGY — SURVEILLANCE VISIT

ATTENDING: [Gynecologic Oncologist]
VISIT TYPE: Established patient, routine surveillance

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT / REASON FOR VISIT:
Ms. Okonkwo is a 59-year-old woman with FIGO IIIC high-grade serous ovarian carcinoma (HGSOC), germline BRCA1-positive, HRD+, presenting for routine surveillance visit at approximately 12 months of niraparib (Zejula) maintenance therapy. She reports she is doing reasonably well overall.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:
Ms. Okonkwo initially presented with stage IIIC HGSOC and underwent primary debulking surgery (PDS) with R0 resection. Final pathology confirmed HGSOC with STIC and positive lymph nodes. She completed 6 cycles of carboplatin/paclitaxel (Taxol) first-line chemotherapy — response confirmed on imaging after cycle 3 and at completion of treatment — with residual grade 2 peripheral neuropathy at the end of therapy for which duloxetine was initiated. She transitioned to Zejula 200 mg daily as maintenance per PRIMA/SOLO-1 rationale, given her BRCA1 germline status.

Early in the maintenance course, she developed grade 3 thrombocytopenia requiring dose hold, with resumption at 100 mg daily. Subsequent CBC monitoring showed recurrent grade 2 thrombocytopenia; bone marrow biopsy was performed and confirmed drug-induced etiology without evidence of dysplasia or recurrence. Dose has remained at Zejula 100 mg daily since that time. At her 6-month maintenance check, CT imaging showed no evidence of disease and CA-125 was 21 U/mL. She also developed grade 2 hypertension during maintenance, managed with uptitration of lisinopril and addition of amlodipine — BP has been better controlled since.

Today she presents for her 12-month maintenance surveillance visit. She reports continued fatigue as the most burdensome symptom, which she rates 4-5/10 at baseline. She denies new or worsening nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain, or bowel changes. Neuropathy symptoms (numbness/tingling bilateral feet) have been stable at a mild level — she notes it does not significantly interfere with daily activities. No bleeding or bruising beyond occasional easy bruising. No headaches, dizziness, or palpitations. No dyspnea on exertion beyond her baseline. She has been adherent to Zejula 100 mg daily and takes it consistently in the evening with food to minimize GI side effects.

RE: genetic counseling — Ms. Okonkwo reports that both of her two adult daughters have completed cascade BRCA1 testing through the genetics clinic. She is awaiting results; she is understandably anxious about this.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS:
Positive: fatigue (chronic, stable), mild bilateral foot numbness (stable), occasional mild nausea (managed), easy bruising.
Negative: no new abdominal or pelvic pain, no bloating, no early satiety, no bowel or bladder changes, no vaginal bleeding, no shortness of breath, no chest pain, no headaches, no visual changes, no new lymphadenopathy noted by patient, no weight loss. Appetite intact. Sleep mildly disrupted by fatigue/restlessness but manageable.

---

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY:
- FIGO IIIC HGSOC, germline BRCA1+ (as above)
- Hypertension — now on dual antihypertensive therapy
- Chemotherapy-induced peripheral neuropathy (CIPN), grade 1 residual
- Drug-induced thrombocytopenia on niraparib
- Remote appendectomy

SOCIAL HISTORY:
Non-smoker, occasional glass of wine socially (currently abstaining). Lives with husband. Three adult children. Works part-time (administrative role); fatigue has limited her hours. West African descent. Postmenopausal.

FAMILY HISTORY:
Mother: breast cancer dx age 45. Maternal aunt: ovarian cancer. Germline BRCA1 pathogenic variant confirmed in patient; family cascade testing underway for daughters.

ALLERGIES: NKDA.

MEDICATIONS:
1. Niraparib (Zejula) 100 mg PO daily (evening, with food)
2. Lisinopril 20 mg PO daily (uptitrated from prior dose)
3. Amlodipine 5 mg PO daily (added for HTN control during maintenance)
4. Duloxetine 60 mg PO daily (CIPN/neuropathic pain)
5. Multivitamin daily

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:
General: Well-appearing, mildly fatigued-appearing woman in no acute distress. Alert and conversational.
Vitals: BP 134/84 mmHg, HR 72 bpm, RR 14, O2 sat 98% RA, Weight stable (noted minor 1 kg loss from last visit — not clinically concerning).
ECOG PS: 1.
Head/Neck: No lymphadenopathy in cervical or supraclavicular chains. Anicteric.
Chest: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheezes or rales.
Cardiovascular: RRR, no murmurs. No peripheral edema.
Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. No palpable organomegaly. Surgical scar well-healed, midline. Bowel sounds normal.
Pelvic (limited exam): No palpable pelvic or adnexal masses. No vaginal cuff tenderness.
Extremities: No edema. Mild decreased sensation to light touch bilateral distal feet — stable from prior exam. No ecchymoses on exam today.
Skin: No rash or petechiae.
Neuro: Grossly intact; ambulates without assistance.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS (today):
CA-125: 19 U/mL (normal; stable from 21 at 6-month check, 18 at end of chemo)
CBC:
  - WBC: 5.2 K/uL (normal)
  - Hgb: 11.0 g/dL (low, grade 1 anemia; stable)
  - Plt: 132 K/uL (low-normal/grade 1; stable, improved from nadir)
  - ANC: 2.8 K/uL (adequate)
BMP: Creatinine 0.9, BUN 14, lytes WNL. No renal concerns.
LFTs: WNL.

---

IMAGING:
No imaging obtained at today's visit. Per our surveillance protocol, cross-sectional imaging (CT chest/abdomen/pelvis) is planned at approximately 6 months from last scan (i.e., ~15 months of maintenance). Prior CT at 6-month maintenance mark showed no evidence of disease, CA-125 consistent.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN:

1. FIGO IIIC HGSOC, germline BRCA1+, HRD+ — currently on Zejula maintenance, ~12 months:
   - Patient continues to demonstrate an excellent biochemical and radiographic response. CA-125 at 19, stable and well within normal limits. No clinical signs or symptoms suggestive of recurrence on today's evaluation.
   - I reviewed the PRIMA and SOLO-1 data with the patient again today, including the landmark PFS benefit seen in the BRCA-mutated subgroup. She understands the goal of maintenance is to prolong the treatment-free interval and ultimately delay recurrence. She is motivated to continue.
   - Continue Zejula 100 mg PO daily. No dose adjustment indicated at this time given stable grade 1 thrombocytopenia and acceptable tolerability.
   - Repeat CBC in 4 weeks, then every 6-8 weeks while on maintenance.
   - Next CT C/A/P in approximately 3 months (15-month maintenance timepoint).
   - CA-125 to be repeated in 3 months or sooner if symptoms arise.

2. Drug-induced thrombocytopenia / hematologic toxicity on niraparib:
   - Plt 132 today — grade 1, stable. No active bleeding. BMBx previously confirmed drug-induced etiology, no dysplasia.
   - Will continue monitoring. If Plt drops below 100, will hold Zejula per protocol and reassess. No further BMBx planned unless counts worsen or there is a change in clinical picture.
   - Discussed that she should report any unusual bruising, petechiae, or bleeding promptly.

3. Hypertension:
   - BP 134/84 today — improved from prior grade 2 readings; now grade 1. Lisinopril 20 mg and amlodipine 5 mg continued. She is adherent.
   - Encouraged home BP monitoring. Referral to PCP for ongoing HTN management co-management, as I want her internist to be in the loop given she is on dual therapy.

4. Peripheral neuropathy (CIPN), grade 1 residual:
   - Stable at grade 1. Duloxetine continued. No dose change needed.
   - She is aware that some degree of residual neuropathy may persist long-term; we discussed physical therapy/foot care. PT referral offered — patient declined at this time, will reconsider.

5. Fatigue:
   - Most bothersome symptom. Multifactorial: anemia (mild), ongoing PARP inhibitor therapy, post-treatment deconditioning.
   - Recommended structured, gentle aerobic activity (walking 20-30 min daily if tolerated). Sleep hygiene reviewed.
   - Hgb 11.0 — not at threshold for intervention; will continue to monitor. Iron studies not rechecked today; ordered for next CBC draw.
   - Consider referral to integrative oncology or cancer rehabilitation if fatigue worsens or fails to improve.

6. Genetic counseling / family cascade testing:
   - Genetic counseling completed. Patient reports both adult daughters have now undergone cascade BRCA1 germline testing. Results pending per patient report. She expressed significant anxiety about this.
   - I provided reassurance and directed her to follow up with our genetics team directly for results disclosure and support. Offered referral to psychology/social work if anxiety becomes a significant issue.
   - Discussed that if daughters test positive, they have options including enhanced screening, risk-reducing interventions, etc. — but this is for the genetics team to counsel.

7. Quality of life / psychosocial:
   - Patient rates QoL as moderate. Fatigue and some anxiety about recurrence remain issues. She is working part-time and managing at home.
   - Continued emotional support offered. Open to social work referral if desired in the future.

FOLLOW-UP: Return in 3 months for next surveillance visit with CT imaging and labs. Sooner if any new symptoms — particularly new pelvic/abdominal pain, bloating, or rising CA-125.

Time spent: ~40 minutes, >50% counseling and coordination of care.

— Electronically signed, [Gynecologic Oncologist, MD]
   Gynecologic Oncology
